message.content:
The salt had long since carved its way into the lines of Elias Thorne’s face, matching the gray of the storm clouds that perpetually bruised the horizon. For forty years, Elias had been the solitary sentinel of Blackwood Point, a jagged tooth of rock jutting out into the churning Atlantic. His life was a metronome of routine: polish the lens, trim the wick, log the weather, and watch the ships.

He knew every contour of the coastline, every shifting sandbar, and every groan of the gale. He knew, too, that he was forgotten. The automated beacons on the mainland had rendered his position obsolete, though the maritime authority refused to decommission him, citing tradition. Tradition, Elias thought, was just a fancy word for stubbornness.

It was on a Tuesday, during a particularly vicious squall, that the bottle washed up.

It wasn’t a glass bottle, but a small, cylindrical tube of brass, sealed with wax that had turned the color of dried blood. It was wedged tight in the crevice of the rocks below the lighthouse gallery, battered by the waves but intact. Elias retrieved it with trembling hands, not from fear, but from a sudden, sharp curiosity that had been dormant for decades.

He carried it into the lantern room, the warm glow of the Fresnel lens reflecting off the brass. With a pocket knife, he pried off the seal. Inside was a single sheet of parchment, yellowed and brittle. The handwriting was elegant, looping, and unmistakably old.

*To the Keeper of Blackwood Point,*

*If you are reading this, the tide has finally brought my secret to you. I am Captain Silas Vane, and I was not lost at sea. I was hidden. Beneath the third rock from the left, when facing the lighthouse
